course_description: This course examines Australian national security law in a global context and critiques its justifications, operation, impacts, and future directions. In doing so, it engages a range of areas, including, criminal justice, court process, civil ‘preventive’ measures, fundamental rights and freedoms, constitutional law, police and intelligence powers, migration, and more.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims:
This course introduces themes and challenges in the field of national security law, and aims to equip students to:

· Understand, critically analyse and communicate complex issues of contemporary significance

· Engage in focused, intellectual, productive debate

· Appreciate and apply key themes and principles relevant to the development of national security law

· Make directed and informed recommendations for or against reform

· Understand key governmental powers and agencies in the national security arena.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and explain key national security laws and approaches.
Identify and explain relevant human rights and constitutional principles and the mechanisms by which they are protected.
Identify and discuss key legal and political processes, actors and challenges in the field of national security law.
Conduct and communicate research in national security law, applying appropriate research principle and methods.
Investigate and critically analyse complex information, problems and concepts in relation to national security.
Independently critique and evaluate the justifications, effectiveness and impacts of national security law from a principled perspective.

assessment_summary.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessment_summary.title: Weekly Hand-Ins

assessment_details.title: Weekly Hand-Ins
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessment_details.task_description: Your weekly Workshop Guide will include an exercise. These exercises are designed to guide your reading and to prepare you for what we are going to do in class. So, it is recommended that you complete the exercise every week. Five of those exercises will be assessed . It is up to you to keep track of how many you have handed in for assessment and to double-check with the course coordinator that you have had 5 assessed by the end of semester. There are 10 Workshops over the course of the Semester, so you will have 10 opportunities to hand-in your answers. You only need to hand in 5 of these weekly exercises, each of which will be graded out of 2 (adding up to a total of 10 marks by the end of the Semester). Those 2 marks will be awarded on the basis of the following Marking Criteria : A complete response to the assigned task, adhering to all instructions. Evidence of independent and genuine engagement with the task including appropriate pre-reading and research. Clear and competent written communication skills. This assessment item will only be accepted in-class and in-person , and cannot be handed in by someone else. Please remember to write your name and student number on your hand-in (writing your name on it makes it easier to hand back in class the following week). This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission must be in-class and in-person.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
There are 10 opportunities to hand in an exercise, and only 5 exercises need to be handed in. As such, there is no allowance for late submissions, extensions or deferrals.
Late submission
No late submissions accepted.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: In-semester Examination
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: An In-Semester Exam will be held after 6pm on 9 September 2025. The exam will be open book (any written or printed material is permitted in the exam; it may also be annotated) and will examine material covered in Weeks 1 to 5 of the course. More detailed information will be provided on Blackboard and in Lectures and Workshops during Semester. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Research Essay
assessment_details.task_description: The primary piece of assessment for this course is a Research Essay of maximum 2,800 words excluding footnote references engaging in independent critical analysis in the field of terrorism law and human rights. You will have the opportunity to select a topic for your Research Essay from a list provided during the mid-semester break. That list and further information and instructions will appear on Blackboard. The grade for the Research Essay will be based upon the following Marking Criteria : Knowledge and Understanding: Demonstrated comprehension, knowledge and understanding of the research topic, including clear identification and explanation of laws, rights and principles as relevant to the research question. Research and Referencing: Evidence of independent research and use of materials, supported by comprehensive referencing (in accordance with the Australian Guide to Legal Citation). Critical Analysis: Evidence of substantial independent critical thinking, originality and insight in relation to the research question. Analysis should be balanced and engage with counterarguments. Reasoned Conclusion: Clear and persuasive conclusions in response to the research question. Writing Skills: Clear communication of information and ideas including a logical structure and scholarly written expression. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The assignment must be submitted electronically via the assessment submission link on the course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) site.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension to Assessment Due Date
Where an extension for an assessment item due within the teaching period in which the course is offered is available, any extension is limited to one week (7 days). In exceptional circumstances, extensions may be granted for more than one week, but in no case will an extension exceed four weeks (28 days) in total. Where a student is incapacitated for a period exceeding four weeks of the teaching period, they should apply for removal of course.
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply . Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
Applications to be submitted before the due date : Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment . Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to accept the request for consideration.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Penalties for Late Submission
The following penalties apply to late submission of an assessment item, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).
First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof) - 10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item. For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks.
More than 7 periods of 24 hours - 100%.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 12 (28 Jul - 26 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Lectures Week 1. National Security Law and Governance Week 2. Political Violence and the Threat Environment Week 3. Rights in the Balance Week 4. Criminal Code s 100.1: Legal Definitions of Terrorism Week 5. Criminal Code Divs 101-102: Crimes of Terror Week 6. Criminal Process: Secret Evidence and the NSI Act Week 7. Preventive Justice: Control Orders (Div 104), Preventive Detention (Div 105), and ASIO’s Special Powers ( ASIO Act Div 3) Week 8. Preventive Justice: Post-Sentence Detention (Div 105A) Week 9. Security, Citizenship and the Constitution Week 10. Criminal Code Pt 5.2: Espionage and Foreign Interference Week 11. Search, Seizure and Surveillance Week 12. Research and Writing on National Security Law and Policy Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 12 (04 Aug - 26 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Workshops Week 2. Terrorism Week 3. Necessary, Effective, Appropriate? Week 4. Defining Terror Week 5. Terror Trials Week 6. Criminal Process (for Terrorists): From Investigation to Post-Sentence Release (or Not) Week 7. Are Preventive Constraints (without Conviction) Necessary, Effective and Appropriate? Week 8. Are Continuing Detention Orders Necessary, Effective and Appropriate? Week 9. Foreign Fighters: Migration Controls for Security Week 10. Espionage and Foreign Interference: Scope and Application Week 11. Free Speech and Democracy in a Surveillance-State Week 12. Essay Workshop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
